name: Sets, Venn Diagrams and Logic
whatIsIt: A SET is a collection of things (its ELEMENTS), written in braces: {2, 4, 6}. UNION (A ∪ B) puts BOTH sets together (everything in either). INTERSECTION (A ∩ B) is only what's in BOTH. A VENN DIAGRAM shows this with overlapping circles. In LOGIC, an IF-THEN statement claims one thing leads to another; a single COUNTEREXAMPLE (one case where it fails) proves it false.
whyNeeded: Sets organise and compare groups — students who play both cricket and chess, numbers that are both even and prime. Logic and counterexamples are the foundation of proof, computer science and clear reasoning.
realLifeUses: Students who like BOTH football and music (intersection)
All students who like EITHER activity (union)
Sorting numbers that are multiples of both 2 and 3
Disproving a claim with one counterexample

vocabulary.meaning: A member of a set (3 is an element of {1, 3, 5}).
Everything in either set (∪) / only what's in both (∩).
One case that makes a general claim false.

story.title: The Club Overlap
story.text: Set A = students in the Music club = {Ria, Sam, Ved}. Set B = Sports club = {Sam, Ved, Ivy}. 'Who is in BOTH?' asks the teacher. Ravi finds the intersection: Sam and Ved. 'And in EITHER?' The union: Ria, Sam, Ved, Ivy. Then the teacher claims 'all primes are odd.' Ravi replies '2 is prime and even — counterexample!'
story.extractedProblem: A = {Ria, Sam, Ved}, B = {Sam, Ved, Ivy}. Who is in BOTH clubs (intersection)?
story.answerInStory: Sam and Ved — the elements common to both sets.

sequences.label: A ∪ B; the 🤝 are A ∩ B

visual.caption: Two sets: union is everything shown; intersection (the shared members 🤝) is only what's in both circles.

teachingGallery.title: Union, intersection, disprove
teachingGallery.note: ∪ = either; ∩ = both; one counterexample disproves a claim.

standardMethod.summary: List each set's elements; union = combine (no repeats), intersection = only shared elements; disprove a claim with one counterexample.
steps: Write each set in braces { } and list its elements.
UNION A ∪ B: list everything in A or B, each element once.
INTERSECTION A ∩ B: list only the elements in BOTH.
Logic: to disprove an 'all…' statement, find ONE example where it fails (a counterexample).
3 is in both; 4 is in both.

workedExamples.problem: Disprove: 'every number ending in 5 is prime.'
steps: 15 is a counterexample — the claim is false.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the intersection when the union is asked (or the reverse).
commonMistakes.fix: Union (∪) = in EITHER set (everything together). Intersection (∩) = in BOTH sets (only the overlap).

commonMistakes.mistake: Trying to prove an 'all' claim by giving examples that fit.
commonMistakes.fix: Examples can't prove 'all'. To DISPROVE it, you need ONE case that fails — a counterexample.

medium.q: To DISPROVE 'all birds can fly' you give…

challenge.q: A = multiples of 2 up to 6 = {2,4,6}; B = multiples of 3 up to 6 = {3,6}. A ∩ B = ?
Ravi tries to prove 'all primes are odd' by listing 3, 5, 7. What's the flaw?

options.label: 2 is an even prime — a counterexample

challenge.answer: 2 is an even prime — a counterexample

questions.q: One case that makes a general claim false is a…

teachBackPrompt: Make two real sets with a grown-up (like 'people who like tea' and 'people who like coffee' in your family). Show the union (either) and intersection (both), and disprove a fun 'all…' claim with a single counterexample.

revisionCard.summary: Set = collection of elements { }. Union A ∪ B = in EITHER set (all together, once). Intersection A ∩ B = in BOTH (the overlap). Venn diagrams show this with circles. Logic: a general 'all' claim is disproved by ONE counterexample.

rememberIt.hook: Set = a collection. Union (OR, all), Intersection (AND, shared)
rememberIt.unpack: A U B = everything in either. A n B = only what is in both. Venn diagrams show it - AND = the overlap.

studentQuestions.a: Union (U) is EVERYTHING in either set (OR). Intersection (n) is only the SHARED part (AND).

studentQuestions.q: What does a Venn diagram show?
studentQuestions.a: Overlapping circles: the overlap is the intersection (in both); the whole is the union.
